---
title: ""
page-layout: full
title-block-banner: false
format:
  html:
    page-layout: full
    toc: false
---

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: landing-container
```{=html}
<!-- 
Hero panel: The Hook
-->
```

:::::::: {.landing-panel .panel-light}
::::::: panel-content
::: hero-title
In an era of systemic disruption
:::

::: hero-subtitle
How do institutions respond to conflict, mistrust, and uncertainty?
:::

::: hero-panel-content
Across business and the public sector, the ground is shifting under our feet. We can no longer count on cohesive societies, trust in authority, or a stable rules-based order. The old models of science, health, and government no longer fit the problems they face. Going back is not an option, but neither is abandoning hope that we can build cohesion, trust, and shared understanding.

My name is Thomas J. Hodges. My research addresses emerging challenges through open science, interdisciplinarity, and enagement with communities, businesses, and government. 

:::

[About](/#about){.btn .btn-outline-primary}
[Research](/#research){.btn .btn-outline-primary}
[Contact](/#contact){.btn .btn-outline-primary}
[Blog](/blog/index.qmd){.btn .btn-outline-primary}
:::::::
::::::::

```{=html}
<!-- 
About Me
-->
```

:::::::: {.landing-panel .panel-muted #about}
::::::: {.panel-content .panel-two-column}
::::: panel-text
::: research-panel-title
About me
:::

::: research-panel-content
I am Thomas J. Hodges, an interdisciplinary social and behavioral scientist based in Atlanta, Georgia, USA.

My research spans academic disciplines, including international relations, psychology, and conflict resolution. I approach research with a focus on transparency, public engagement, and applied impact.

During my PhD studies, I helped found the AMES Research Center to address the mental health crisis in Georgia. In 2025, we were awarded the Public Impact Research Award for North America by the APLU.

My work has been published in journals such as *Peace and Conflict*, *Aggression and Violent Behavior*, *Traumatology*, *Military Psychology*, *Peace Chronicle*, and the *Journal of Community Engagement and Scholarship*.


[My CV](files/curriculum-vitae.pdf){.btn .btn-outline-primary}
[Resources for Students](blog/#category=Resources%20for%20Students){.btn .btn-outline-primary}
:::
:::::

::: panel-image
![](/files/profiles/headshot-square-edit.png)
:::
:::::::
::::::::


```{=html}
<!-- 
MY RESEARCH
-->
```

:::::::: {.landing-panel .panel-accent #research}
::: eyebrow
Research
:::

:::::: panel-content
::: research-panel-title
My Research
:::

:::: research-panel-content
My research examines ways that institutions respond to conflict, mistrust, and uncertainty in an era of systemic disruption.

Currently, I am focusing on three areas:

1.  [Conflict, Trust, and American Health](/#health)

2.  [Conflict and Risk in the Era of Economic Statecraft](/#economic-statecraft)

3.  [Quantitative Conflict Research: Bayesian Methods, Vocation, and Transparency in Peace and Conflict Studies](/#bayes)

Each of these areas addresses the emerging challenges of the 21st century. Whether it is health, business, or academia, the old models that relied on trust, stability, and authority have broken down. Mistrust of healthcare is rampant; the rules-based order of globalization has given way to competition; and academia is under attack from critics who espouse anti-intellectualism. 

These emerging challenges require emerging solution: new ways of thinking and doing that resolve underlying issues and take seriously the drivers of conflict and division. 

[Read more about my research portfolio](/research/){.btn .btn-outline-primary}
[See all of my articles, presentations, and projects](/research/){.btn .btn-outline-primary}
::::
::::::
::::::::

```{=html}
<!-- 
RESEARCH PANEL 1
-->
```

::::::: {.landing-panel .panel-muted #health}
::: eyebrow
Research
:::

::::: panel-content
::: research-panel-title
Conflict, Trust, and American Health
:::

::: research-panel-content
In the United States, healthy skepticism of medicine has turned to unhealthy mistrust. The rise of the Make American Healthy Again (MAHA) movement has drawn on conspiracy theories to undermine public health institutions. Yet underlying MAHA's concerns are real issues with American health: a history of bias, worsening health among children, and a healthcare system that enriches insurers and pharmaceutical companies at the expense of patients.

While conspiracy theories will not solve the real issues, the energy to “do something” exists. Conflict resolution has a pivotal role to play, helping channel energy toward rebuilding trust, changing how patients and providers relate to each other, and ultimately reforming the deeper issues with the American health system. 

My research examines medical mistrust from a conflict resolution perspective. It develops and evaluates interventions to address medical mistrust that are rooted in conflict resolution processes.

This research agenda builds on my prior studies of mistrust, stigma, and mental health, as well as applied conflict interventions with communities.  

[Read more about this research agenda](/research/health-agenda/index.qmd){.btn .btn-outline-primary}
[See my related research](/research/#category=Health){.btn .btn-outline-primary}
:::
:::::
:::::::

```{=html}
<!-- 
RESEARCH PANEL 2
-->
```

::::::: {.landing-panel .panel-light #economic-statecraft}
::: eyebrow
Research
:::

::::: panel-content
::: research-panel-title
Conflict and Risk in the Era of Economic Statecraft
:::

::: research-panel-content
A research agenda on how geopolitical competition, sanctions, supply chains, industrial policy, and economic statecraft shape organizational and strategic risk.

[Read more about this research agenda](/research/economic-statecraft-agenda/index.qmd){.btn .btn-outline-primary}
[See my related research](/research/#category=Economic%20Statecraft){.btn .btn-outline-primary}
:::
:::::
:::::::

```{=html}
<!-- 
NEW PANEL
-->
```

::::::: {.landing-panel .panel-muted #bayes}
::: eyebrow
Research
:::

::::: panel-content
::: research-panel-title
Quantitative Conflict Research
:::

::: research-panel-content
An introductory textbook on Bayesian analysis for peace and conflict doctoral students. More than stats, I focus on developing scholars with a sense of vocation, an ethics of transparency, and an ability to change the academy for the better.

[Read more about this project](/research/bayesian-textbook/index.qmd){.btn .btn-outline-primary}
[See my related research](/research/){.btn .btn-outline-primary}
[Read the first chapter](files/bayesian-textbook-chapter-1.pdf){.btn .btn-outline-primary} 
:::
:::::
:::::::

```{=html}
<!-- 
CONTACT INFO
-->
```

:::::: {.landing-panel .panel-light #contact}
::::: panel-content
::: research-panel-title
Contact
:::

::: research-panel-content
If you have questions, comments, or ideas about my research, I want to talk to you. Feel free to send me an email or connect on social media. 

::: {.contact-buttons}

[{{< iconify bi envelope size=1.2em title="E-mail" >}} E-mail](mailto:tommyjhodges@gmail.com){.contact-button}

[{{< iconify bi github size=1.2em title="GitHub" >}} GitHub](https://github.com/TJH-RESEARCH){.contact-button rel="me"}

[{{< iconify bi linkedin size=1.2em title="LinkedIn" >}} LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/tom-hodges){.contact-button rel="me"}

[{{< iconify fa-brands:researchgate size=1.2em title="ResearchGate" >}} ResearchGate](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Thomas-Hodges-4?ev=hdr_xprf){.contact-button rel="me"}

[{{< ai google-scholar size=1.2em title="Google Scholar" >}} Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/citations?hl=en&authuser=3&user=wG3FltQAAAAJ){.contact-button rel="me"}

[{{< ai orcid size=1.2em title="ORCID" >}} ORCID](https://orcid.org/0000-0002-5184-7346){.contact-button rel="me"}

:::

:::
:::::
::::::

```{=html}
<!-- 
END .landing-container
-->
```
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
